title: Variables and strings
description: Store values in variables and stitch strings together with template literals.
objectives: Declare variables with let and const
Join strings with + and template literals
Understand when to reassign a value vs keep it fixed

completionRules.expected: Hi, I'm Ada and I'm 36 years old.

recap: Variables give values names. Use `const` when the name shouldn't be reassigned, `let` when it should. Template literals (backticks) let you drop variables right inside a string with `${name}` instead of splicing them with +.
practicePrompts: Store three of your favorite things in variables and print them on one line.
Try reassigning a `let` variable after you first set it — what does the output show?

practiceExercises.title: Full name
practiceExercises.prompt: Create two variables `first` and `last` holding any first and last name. Print them joined by a space — for example, `Ada Lovelace`.
practiceExercises.goal: Join two string variables into one output line.
practiceExercises.starterCode: // Create first and last, then print them with a space between
hints: Two strings plus a literal space character in between.
You can join them with `+ " " +` or use a template literal with a space inside.

practiceExercises.title: Two times the price
practiceExercises.prompt: Create a number variable `price` set to 12. Print a sentence that includes both the price and twice the price, like: `Price: 12, Double: 24`.
practiceExercises.goal: Do simple math with a variable and include the result in a string.
practiceExercises.starterCode: // Declare price and print the sentence
hints: Template literals work with numbers too — you can embed any expression.

practiceExercises.title: Counter step
practiceExercises.prompt: Use `let` to create a variable `count` starting at 0. Print it, increase it by 1, print it again, increase it by 1, print it again. Output must be `0`, `1`, `2` on three separate lines.
practiceExercises.goal: Reassign a variable and see the change reflected in the output.
practiceExercises.starterCode: // Declare count = 0 with let, then print/increment three times
hints: `count = count + 1;` bumps the value by one. `count += 1;` is a shortcut.
`const` can't be reassigned — that's why this exercise needs `let`.
Three console.log calls, each after the value changes.
